turns.content: okay so i just finished the most incredible podcast series and i need to tell somebody about it because none of the guys at the truck stop care about history
Ha! I'm all ears — what was the series about?

the haitian revolution man like the actual full story not the little paragraph they give you in school if they even mention it at all it was this podcast called revolutions by mike duncan and he did a whole season on it like 30 something episodes going deep into everything from the slave trade to toussaint louverture to dessalines and the whole thing
Oh Mike Duncan's Revolutions is fantastic! His Haitian Revolution season is considered one of the best things he's ever done. What hit you the hardest about it?

man where do i even start like the fact that enslaved people in saint domingue rose up and defeated napoleon bonapartes army which was supposed to be the most powerful military in the world at the time and they did it through sheer will and strategy and sacrifice like toussaint louverture was a genius he played the french and the spanish and the british against each other while building an army out of nothing

Toussaint was an incredible strategist — self-educated, diplomatic, and militarily brilliant. The way he navigated between three European empires while holding the revolution together is one of the most underappreciated stories in world history. Did the podcast go into his capture and imprisonment in France?

yes and that part messed me up because napoleon basically tricked him into a meeting and then arrested him and shipped him to this cold prison in the jura mountains in france and he died there alone in a cell and he never got to see haiti become free which happened a year after he died and its so tragic you know like the man who made it all possible didnt get to see the finish line

That's one of the most heartbreaking parts of the story. He literally built the foundation for the first free Black republic in the Western Hemisphere and died in a freezing cell thousands of miles away. His famous quote about it is haunting too — something about how they'd only cut down the trunk of the tree of liberty, and it would grow back from the roots.

yes exactly that quote gave me chills when they read it on the podcast and then dessalines he was the one who actually finished it and declared independence on january 1 1804 and man when duncan described that moment on the podcast i was driving through south carolina at like 2am and i literally had tears in my eyes because this is my history you know this is where i come from and most people in america have no idea it even happened

That must hit completely differently when it's your own heritage. January 1, 1804 — Haiti became the first country founded by formerly enslaved people, and it happened decades before abolition in the US or the British Empire. The fact that this story isn't widely taught is a crime.

Was there anything in the podcast that surprised you, that you didn't already know from growing up?

yeah actually the part about how the revolution affected the rest of the world like it scared slave owners everywhere and it directly led to napoleon selling louisiana to the united states because he lost haiti and didnt need louisiana anymore so without the haitian revolution america would be a completely different shape like literally the map would be different and nobody connects those dots

That's one of the biggest untold connections in history! The Louisiana Purchase — which doubled the size of the United States — happened directly because Haitian revolutionaries defeated Napoleon's plans for a Caribbean empire. Without that, there's no westward expansion as we know it. It's wild how central Haiti's story is to American history and how rarely that connection is made.

right and then the other part that hit me was how after independence the world punished haiti for daring to be free like france made them pay 150 million francs which was an insane amount of money basically reparations in reverse like you have to pay us for freeing yourselves and haiti was paying that debt until 1947 and thats a huge part of why the country has struggled economically ever since and when i think about that and then i think about my family leaving after the earthquake it all connects you know its all one long story

That's a devastating throughline — from the "independence debt" that crippled Haiti's economy for over 140 years, to the poverty that made the 2010 earthquake so catastrophic, to your family's journey. When you lay it out like that, it's not ancient history at all. It's a direct line from 1804 to your life right now.

Do you think you'll share the podcast with anyone? It sounds like the kind of thing more people need to hear.

i already told my cousin marc about it and he said he would listen but knowing him he will forget and honestly i want to make naomi listen to it when shes a little older because she needs to know this story you know she needs to know where her family comes from and what our people did and that we have a history of being strong and fighting for freedom not just the earthquake and the poverty which is all most americans think about when they hear haiti

That's a beautiful reason to share it. Naomi growing up knowing the full story — not just the struggles but the strength, the brilliance, the revolution that changed the world — that gives her something to carry with pride. When she's old enough to understand it, hearing Toussaint's story and Dessalines' story is going to mean so much coming from her dad.

Any other podcast series on your list now that you've finished this one?

man mike duncan also did the french revolution and the mexican revolution and i think im going to do those next but honestly nothing is going to hit like the haiti one did just because its personal you know like when dessalines ripped the white out of the french flag and made the blue and red haitian flag i was like thats my flag thats the flag my grandmother had on her wall alright i gotta go im parked up for the night and im about to rewatch some youtube videos about toussaint because apparently im obsessed now sa k pase

Ha! Nothing wrong with a healthy obsession with your own history. Mike Duncan's French Revolution season is excellent too, but you're right — the Haiti one hits different when it's personal. Enjoy the YouTube rabbit hole tonight, and maybe start making a playlist for when Naomi's ready. Sa k pase, have a good night!
